states.name: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu

maintenance.law: Section 125 CrPC / Section 20 of DV Act, 2005
maintenance.summary: You can claim monthly financial support from your spouse if you are unable to maintain yourself.
steps: Visit the Family Court in your district (or Judicial Magistrate Court if no Family Court exists).
Obtain the maintenance application form from the filing counter.
Attach marriage proof, income details of both parties, and your financial needs statement.
Pay the nominal court fee at the counter and collect your case number.
Attend the first hearing date mentioned on your receipt.
maintenance.timeline: First hearing within 30–60 days; interim maintenance may be granted earlier.
maintenance.fees: Nominal — typically ₹50–₹500 depending on claim amount.

domestic-violence.courtType: Magistrate Court (Protection Officer route also available)
domestic-violence.law: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
domestic-violence.summary: You are protected from physical, emotional, sexual, and economic abuse within the home.
steps: Contact the Protection Officer in your district or dial 181 (Women Helpline) for immediate help.
File an application before the Magistrate under the DV Act.
Describe incidents with dates and attach evidence (medical reports, photos, messages).
Request protection order, residence order, or monetary relief as needed.
Attend the ex-parte or regular hearing as scheduled.
documents: Evidence of abuse (medical reports, FIR copy, photos)
domestic-violence.timeline: Urgent ex-parte orders possible within days; full hearing in 60–90 days.
domestic-violence.fees: No court fee for filing under DV Act.

divorce.law: Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 / Special Marriage Act, 1954
divorce.summary: Legal dissolution of marriage — mutual consent or contested divorce.
steps: Visit the Family Court in your district.
File divorce petition (mutual or contested) with grounds stated.
For mutual consent: both parties must appear; 6-month cooling period may apply.
Attend counselling session if ordered by the court.
Obtain the final decree after all hearings.

dowry.summary: Criminal complaint for dowry demand, harassment, or cruelty.
steps: File an FIR at your local police station under Section 498A IPC.
Keep a copy of the FIR and daily diary (DD) number.
Apply for protection or residence orders under DV Act if needed.
Cooperate with the investigating officer and provide evidence.
Follow up in court when the charge sheet is filed.
documents: List of dowry items and demands
dowry.timeline: FIR immediate; investigation 60–90 days; trial varies.

custody.law: Guardian and Wards Act / Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act
custody.summary: Petition for child custody, visitation rights, or guardianship.
steps: File custody petition in the Family Court of the district where the child resides.
State why you are the better guardian for the child's welfare.
Attend mediation or counselling if ordered.
Attend final hearing for permanent custody order.
documents: Evidence of your ability to care for the child
custody.timeline: Interim orders in 30–60 days; final order may take 6–12 months.

property.summary: Claim your share in ancestral or joint family property as a daughter or legal heir.
steps: Consult a lawyer or DLSA for free legal aid (call 15100).
File a partition suit in the Civil Court of the district where property is located.
Attach property documents, succession proof, and family tree.
Request interim injunction if property is being sold.
documents: Succession certificate or legal heir certificate
property.fees: Court fee based on property value — legal aid available.

harassment.law: POSH Act, 2013 (Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace)
harassment.summary: Complaint against sexual harassment at your workplace.
steps: File a written complaint with your employer's Internal Complaints Committee (ICC).
If no ICC or unsatisfied with response, approach the Local Complaints Committee (LCC).
ICC must complete inquiry within 90 days.
If needed, file a case in the Labour Court or civil court for compensation.
Keep copies of all complaints and responses.
documents: Written complaint with incident details and dates
harassment.timeline: ICC inquiry within 90 days; court case timeline varies.

pocso.law: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012
pocso.summary: Protects every child below 18 years from sexual abuse, assault, harassment and pornography.
steps: File an FIR at the nearest police station.
The child's statement should preferably be recorded by a woman police officer.
Medical examination will be conducted without delay.
The case will be transferred to the Special POCSO Court.
pocso.timeline: Investigation generally within 2 months; trial should be completed as early as possible.

child-marriage.courtType: District Court / Child Marriage Prohibition Officer
child-marriage.summary: Marriage involving a girl below 18 years or a boy below 21 years is prohibited.
steps: Report the incident to the Child Marriage Prohibition Officer.
File a complaint before the District Court.
Request cancellation of the marriage if already solemnized.
Seek protection and rehabilitation if required.

child-abuse.law: Juvenile Justice Act, 2015 and POCSO Act
child-abuse.summary: Protection against physical, emotional and sexual abuse of children.

minor-rights.law: Constitution of India, JJ Act, POCSO Act
minor-rights.summary: Minor girls have rights to education, protection, healthcare and safety.
steps: Seek legal aid if rights are violated.
File complaint through Police or District Legal Services Authority.

education-rights.law: Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
education-rights.summary: Children aged 6–14 years have the right to free education.
steps: File complaint before District Education Officer.
Approach State Commission for Protection of Child Rights if needed.

kidnapping.summary: Immediate police action is available in kidnapping cases.

missing-person.summary: Missing women and girls must be reported immediately.

guardianship.summary: Legal appointment of guardian for a minor.

adoption.law: Juvenile Justice Act and CARA Guidelines
adoption.summary: Legal adoption process regulated by CARA.

cyber-stalking.courtType: Cyber Crime Police Station / Magistrate Court
cyber-stalking.law: Information Technology Act, 2000 & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)
cyber-stalking.summary: Repeated online monitoring, unwanted messages, threats, or tracking through social media or digital platforms.
steps: Take screenshots of chats, emails, and profiles.
File a complaint at the nearest Cyber Crime Police Station.
Block the offender but preserve all evidence.
Follow up with the investigating officer.
cyber-stalking.timeline: Complaint registered immediately; investigation usually begins within a few days.

cyber-bullying.law: Information Technology Act, 2000 & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
cyber-bullying.summary: Online bullying, abusive messages, public humiliation, or repeated harassment on digital platforms.
steps: Report abusive accounts on the platform.
Visit the Cyber Crime Police Station if harassment continues.
cyber-bullying.timeline: Investigation depends on the nature of the offence.

online-harassment.law: Information Technology Act & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
online-harassment.summary: Online abuse, threats, obscene messages, or repeated unwanted communication.
steps: File an FIR if threats are serious.

blackmail.courtType: Cyber Crime Police Station / Sessions Court
blackmail.law: Information Technology Act & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
blackmail.summary: Threatening to leak personal information, photographs, or videos for money or other demands.
steps: Visit the nearest Cyber Crime Police Station.
Seek immediate police protection if threatened.

revenge-porn.courtType: Cyber Crime Police Station / Magistrate Court
revenge-porn.law: Information Technology Act, 2000 & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
revenge-porn.summary: Sharing or threatening to share intimate images without consent.
steps: Report the content on the platform immediately.
Approach the Cyber Crime Police Station.
Request immediate removal of the content.
revenge-porn.timeline: Content removal requests are treated urgently.

identity-theft.summary: Unauthorized use of your identity, Aadhaar, PAN, bank details or online accounts.
steps: Inform your bank if financial information is involved.

financial-fraud.law: Information Technology Act & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
financial-fraud.summary: UPI fraud, OTP scams, fake investment schemes, or unauthorized online transactions.
financial-fraud.timeline: Report immediately for better recovery chances.

police-complaint.summary: You can file a police complaint for any cognizable offence affecting you.
steps: Collect the acknowledgement or diary number.
If the offence is cognizable, request FIR registration.
police-complaint.timeline: Same day registration in most cases.

fir-registration.summary: An FIR is mandatory for cognizable offences reported to the police.
steps: Collect a free copy of the FIR.

sexual-harassment.summary: Protection against unwanted sexual advances, physical contact, sexually coloured remarks, or other forms of sexual harassment.
steps: Report the incident to the nearest police station.
File an FIR if the offence is cognizable.
Preserve evidence such as messages, CCTV footage, or witness details.
Attend hearings as directed by the court.
sexual-harassment.timeline: FIR immediately; investigation generally within 60–90 days.

workplace-harassment.courtType: Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) / Local Complaints Committee
workplace-harassment.law: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013
workplace-harassment.summary: Protection against sexual harassment at the workplace.
steps: Submit a written complaint to the ICC within 3 months.
If no ICC exists, approach the Local Complaints Committee.
If dissatisfied, seek legal remedy before the appropriate court.
workplace-harassment.timeline: Inquiry should be completed within 90 days.

rape.summary: Victims of rape or sexual assault have the right to immediate police assistance, medical care, legal aid and compensation.
steps: Visit the nearest police station to register an FIR.
rape.timeline: Immediate police action; trial depends on court schedule.

stalking.summary: Repeatedly following, contacting, monitoring or threatening a woman against her wishes.

acid-attack.summary: Victims of acid attacks are entitled to free medical treatment and compensation.

human-trafficking.law: Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
human-trafficking.summary: Protection against trafficking for exploitation, forced labour or sexual exploitation.

domestic-abuse-child.courtType: Child Welfare Committee / Juvenile Justice Board
domestic-abuse-child.summary: Protection against physical, emotional or sexual abuse of children within the family.

protection-order.law: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
protection-order.summary: Protection Orders prevent the respondent from committing further acts of violence or harassment.
steps: File an application through the Protection Officer or directly before the Magistrate.
protection-order.timeline: Interim protection may be granted within a few days.

residence-rights.law: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
residence-rights.summary: A woman has the right to reside in the shared household even if she does not own it.
steps: File an application before the Magistrate.
residence-rights.timeline: Interim relief may be granted quickly.

streedhan.law: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 & Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita
streedhan.summary: A woman has the legal right to recover her Streedhan (gifts, jewellery, valuables and property received before, during or after marriage).
steps: Prepare a list of Streedhan items.
Collect bills, photographs or witness details if available.
File a complaint before the Magistrate or Family Court.
Seek recovery of property through the court.

inheritance.summary: Women have equal inheritance rights in ancestral and self-acquired property according to applicable personal laws.
steps: Obtain legal heir certificate if required.
File a partition or succession suit.

property-rights.summary: Women have equal rights in property ownership and inheritance under applicable laws.
steps: Consult District Legal Services Authority if needed.
File a civil suit if rights are denied.

family-dispute.summary: Family Courts resolve disputes relating to marriage, maintenance, custody and family matters.
steps: File a petition before the Family Court.
family-dispute.timeline: Several months to a few years.

marriage-registration.law: Hindu Marriage Act / Special Marriage Act
marriage-registration.summary: Registering marriage provides legal proof and simplifies access to legal rights.
steps: Book an appointment with the Marriage Registrar.
marriage-registration.timeline: Usually within a few days to a month.

live-in-rights.law: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
live-in-rights.summary: Women in qualifying live-in relationships may receive legal protection against domestic violence and claim certain reliefs.
steps: File an application under the Domestic Violence Act.
Request appropriate relief from the court.
live-in-rights.fees: No court fee under the DV Act.

legal-aid.summary: Eligible women, children and economically weaker persons can receive free legal assistance.
steps: A lawyer will be assigned if eligible.

medical-negligence.summary: Patients may seek compensation for negligence by healthcare providers.
steps: Seek an expert medical opinion if possible.
File a complaint before the Consumer Commission or Court.
medical-negligence.timeline: Several months to a few years.

maintenance-parents.law: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007
maintenance-parents.summary: Parents and senior citizens can claim maintenance from their children.
steps: File an application before the Maintenance Tribunal.

senior-citizen.law: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007
senior-citizen.summary: Senior citizens are entitled to maintenance and protection from neglect and abuse.

other.courtType: Appropriate Court depending on the matter
other.summary: If your issue does not fall into any listed category, consult the District Legal Services Authority or an advocate to identify the appropriate legal remedy.
steps: Visit the nearest Legal Services Authority or advocate.
File the appropriate petition or complaint.
other.fees: Varies according to the applicable law.

victim-compensation.law: Victim Compensation Scheme under Section 395 BNSS
victim-compensation.summary: Victims of serious crimes, including women and children, may receive financial compensation from the State.
steps: Apply through the DLSA or Court.
Provide proof of expenses and losses.

free-legal-services.summary: Women, children, SC/ST persons, victims of trafficking and economically weaker persons are entitled to free legal services.
steps: A lawyer will be assigned if eligible.

fir-refused.summary: If police refuse to register an FIR, you can approach senior police officers or the Magistrate.
steps: Request the police to provide refusal in writing.
Send a written complaint to the Superintendent of Police.
Approach the Magistrate if no action is taken.

police-inaction.summary: If the police fail to investigate properly, higher authorities or courts can be approached.
steps: Submit a written complaint to the SP.
Approach the Magistrate or High Court if required.

one-stop-centre.summary: Provides integrated support including legal aid, counselling, shelter and medical assistance for women facing violence.
steps: Visit the nearest One Stop Centre.
Get assistance in filing complaints if required.

witness-protection.summary: Witnesses facing threats may request protection from the Court.
steps: Submit an application to the Court.
witness-protection.timeline: Emergency protection may be granted quickly.

mediation.summary: Many family disputes can be resolved through mediation before trial.
steps: Sign settlement if agreement is reached.
mediation.timeline: Usually completed within a few weeks.

appeal.summary: If dissatisfied with a court's decision, you may file an appeal before a higher court within the prescribed limitation period.
steps: Obtain certified copy of the judgment.
File the appeal within the limitation period.

women-helpline.summary: Women facing violence or harassment can seek immediate assistance through government helplines.

up:lucknow.filingCounter: Ground Floor, New Block — Filing Counter No. 3
up:lucknow.legalAid: DLSA Lucknow — Room 12, District Court Campus

bihar:patna.address: High Court Campus Area, Patna, Bihar — 800001

defaultDistrictInfo.address: District Court Complex — confirm exact address at local registry
defaultDistrictInfo.timings: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM (Mon–Fri) — may vary by state
defaultDistrictInfo.filingCounter: Ask at the main gate for the Filing Counter

delhi:new-delhi.legalAid: DLSA New Delhi, Patiala House Court
